title: Lesson 445
transcript: You'll remember us stressing the importance of including security, not only in the software development lifecycle, but the entire lifecycle, which obviously includes more than just development. development typically ends when we put applications and systems into production. But then the job of security doesn't end there, right? We have to be able to maintain the applications and the systems and be able to support things like change management and logging and monitoring and testing and so on. In fact, we said, security has to be involved not only in software development phases, but also the operations And later, at some point when the system or the application has finished its useful life, for example, we want to be able to decommission it and dispose of it properly, which may need security to be involved as well. All right. So in this section of the exam outline, we just re-emphasize that. And these are examples where obviously security has to be involved in supporting different activities that are part of the operations phase of applications and systems and technologies, right? So we need to be involved, for example, security has to be involved in the monitoring aspects of systems and applications and architectures to make sure that they're working properly and always protecting the organization. And on a regular basis, as we discussed in domain six, testing and assessment, for example. we need to be able to do that on a regular basis with really important applications and systems as well. We support initiatives like patching and change management and configuration management and so on. By the way, anytime you see a list, Just like this one, it's not meant to be an all-inclusive complete list of how security in this case is involved in the operations and maintenance phases of the system lifecycle. But these are specifically mentioned in the exam outline, which means you may get some questions related to them. But obviously the theme here is that security on a regular basis has to support the ongoing operations of systems and applications and architectures from the perspective of obviously being able to be involved in some of these things from a security perspective. And that also includes change management. Now, I know we talked about change management already in several places. We talked about change management in domain number one. We talked about change management in domain number seven from the perspective of security has to be involved. in change management and doing changes properly with anything that is already running in production, for example. And so this just reemphasizes that entire point. And so these are obviously the phases involved in change management. We don't have to go through these in detail again, but the bottom line is that security has to be involved right at the start. and throughout each of the phases of what most organizations call change management. Sometimes it's called change control. But this is a rigorous process that allows changes to be made to anything that is already running in production, for example. So obviously here in domain number eight, we're focused on making changes to applications and systems and so on. Whenever a change request comes in, it obviously has to be assessed to understand the ramifications of that change and the impact it's going to have. But this is where security has to be involved right here. In fact, in most organizations, security is part of the change control board or committee that reviews changes that have come in or request for changes that have come in so that we can impact what that change is going to impact from a security perspective is it still going to allow the organization to protect itself you know from a reputational perspective do we have still the ability to be able to meet requirements related to laws and regulations and privacy, compliance, in other words. Can we still protect our assets? Are they going to put, for example, our stakeholders at risk, those changes, right? So security has to be part of the change control board or committee that obviously reviews changes and either approves or denies those changes. Security has to be a big part of that and I know the exam obviously addresses that requirement. And then of course, if the change is approved, then we start going through the different phases of change management, which as we said earlier, and we'll repeat here, needs to involve security. Because each of these steps that are part of change management or change control, would obviously also need to include security. There are specific security things that need to get done at each of these phases that can only be done by the security function and those that are focused on security. The last message here is that it doesn't matter what you're changing. whether you're changing the functionality of an application or a system, or whether you're changing the security controls within that application, or you're changing the configuration parameters that that application is running in, the environment, for example, that it's running in and its configuration, or whether you're doing patch management and applying patches to applications or even operating systems, or you're releasing new versions of things or new features or new applications in general. This all needs to be part of your overall change management process that obviously needs to include security right from the start and throughout each of the phases. That's the message here, obviously focused on security. systems development and making changes to systems and applications that are already running in production.
